experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil, so 0 and 2 excludes known evil and is the best available safe-looking opening team. In team voting, good should approve teams without known evil, especially quest 1 where only one fail is needed and information is limited. Approving supports a likely successful first quest and reveals little harmful information since rejecting my own clean team would be suspicious.
Player 1: I have no concrete role information yet beyond myself being Good. On round 1, with no prior voting or quest data, a 2-player team of [0,2] is as plausible as any. As Good, early game I usually approve the first team to gather information unless there is a strong reason to reject, which there is not. My vote reveals little individually and helps advance the game state to produce quest information.
Player 3: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0 and 2 are both non-evil from my perspective unless one is Merlin/Percival. This is team voting on quest 1, and the proposed team [0,2] contains no evil, so approving gives good a likely clean pass and some trust. As evil, early game I generally want to avoid obviously all-good teams succeeding when I can, and a reject on round 1 reveals little because many players reject opening teams for uncertainty.
Player 4: I know player 3 is my evil teammate, so team [0,2] contains no evil players and would likely succeed, giving good credibility to 0 and 2. On round 1 as evil, I generally want to avoid clean all-good teams passing when possible, especially since approving grants town information and trust. My vote is private except in aggregate, and rejecting a clean team helps prevent good from gaining momentum without exposing much beyond mild suspicion on me.
Player 0: As a Servant on round 1 with no prior information, players 0 and 2 are both unknown except I know myself is Good. A team including me guarantees at least one Good, and on 5-player quest 1 a 2-person team is standard. Early game, approving reasonable opening teams gives info from quest outcome; rejecting without evidence is usually unhelpful. Approving reveals little beyond willingness to test an opening team and helps Good gather information.

This reveals some suspicion on me if the quest fails, but preserving teammate 4 and securing an Evil point is worth it.

Player 2: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,1,3] includes confirmed evil player 3, so as Good I should reject to avoid handing Evil a sabotage opportunity. Rejecting also signals mild suspicion of this team without overtly exposing perfect knowledge; approving would strongly hurt Good.
Player 1: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,2], so 0 and 2 each look somewhat more likely good, though evil can pass early. Player 3 now proposes [0,1,3], keeping 0 and adding me plus 3; this is a reasonable exploratory team for quest 2. As a good Servant in team voting, I should generally approve teams that include at least one previously successful member and avoid over-rejecting early without stronger evidence. My vote reveals mild trust in 0 and willingness to test 3, which is acceptable for good.
Player 4: I know player 3 is my evil teammate, while 0 and 1 are good-unknowns from my perspective. The first quest succeeded with team [0,2], which slightly suggests 0 and 2 look cleaner publicly. Current phase is team voting; as evil Assassin, I want to avoid strongly signaling by rejecting every team with evil on it, and this proposed team includes my teammate 3 plus two players not on the first success. A 3-player team on quest 2 only needs one fail, so if approved, 3 can sabotage. Approving helps evil more than rejecting, and my vote is not too revealing because many players would approve a plausible team after a success.
Player 0: Quest 1 with team [0,2] succeeded, so players 0 and 2 each look somewhat more trustworthy, though one could still be evil and choose pass. Proposed team [0,1,3] keeps me, excludes 2, and adds two unknowns; it's not clearly bad, but it drops one of the only mildly trusted players from the successful team. As a Good Servant in team voting, I should favor teams with stronger evidence. Rejecting pressures for a team including more confirmed-looking players and reveals that I value prior success data rather than blindly approving.
Player 3: I am evil Minion with teammate 4. Quest 1 succeeded on team [0,2], so at least one of 0 or 2 is likely good, though double-evil is impossible since only 4 is my teammate. Current proposed team [0,1,3] includes me but not teammate 4. As evil, I want teams with evil on them approved, and this team has me. Approving is optimal; rejecting my own team with me on it is suspicious. If team goes, I can choose fail on quest 2 to put evil on the board. My vote reveals support for a team containing me, which is normal and not especially harmful.

This reveals less about Player 3 and preserves assassination options later, while maximizing evil win chances.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 4 is evil. Team [1,4] contains a known evil, so as Good I should reject. Previous failed team [0,1,3] had exactly one fail; since 3 is known evil, that fits, while 4 remains independently evil. Rejecting avoids sending a guaranteed evil on quest and reveals a consistent pro-good stance without exposing Merlin-specific knowledge too strongly.
Player 3: I know player 4 is evil with me. Quest 1 team [0,2] succeeded, so 0 and 2 are somewhat cleaner. Quest 2 team [0,1,3] failed with exactly one fail likely from me, which makes 0 and 1 look better to others while increasing suspicion on me somewhat. Current proposed team [1,4] contains my evil teammate and excludes me. As evil in a team vote, I want missions with evil on them to go through, especially a 2-person team where 4 can fail. Approving helps our side and does not reveal much beyond possible coordination, while rejecting risks losing a strong sabotage opportunity.
Player 1: I know I am Good. Quest 1 team [0,2] succeeded, so 0 and 2 look somewhat better. Quest 2 team [0,1,3] had exactly one fail; since I am Good, either 0 or 3 is Evil. That makes a strong pair for Quest 3 likely include me plus one of {2,4}, but [1,4] gives little coverage and excludes the more-cleared 2. As a voter, optimal Good play is to reject teams that are not sufficiently justified, especially early in the round when there is room to seek a better team. Rejecting also signals that this 2-person proposal is under-informed given current evidence.
Player 0: Quest 1 team [0,2] succeeded, so 0 and 2 are somewhat more likely good. Quest 2 team [0,1,3] had exactly one fail, so among 1 and 3 at least one is evil, while I know 0 is good. Proposed team [1,4] excludes both players from the known successful team and includes 1, who is directly implicated by the failed quest. As a Servant, I should reject risky teams that likely contain evil, especially on round 1 where there is no immediate rejection danger. Rejecting also signals suspicion on 1 and the weak evidence around 4.
Player 4: I know player 3 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,1,3] failed with exactly 1 fail, which is consistent with 3 failing and suggests 0 and 1 may be good, though not certain. Current phase is team voting on my proposed team [1,4]. As evil Assassin on a 2-person quest, having myself on the team gives evil control to fail the quest if approved. Approving is optimal because it advances an evil-favorable team. My vote to approve is expected and reveals little beyond supporting my own team.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil, so 0 and 1 are the remaining non-seen players with one of them possibly Mordred. Team [0,2] excludes known evil and previously succeeded on quest 1, making it one of the safest available 2-player teams. As good, I should approve a team that has high success odds and avoid signaling too strongly; rejecting this strong team would create suspicion and hurt good.
Player 0: I know [0,2] previously went on a 2-person quest that succeeded, so both are more likely good than players on the failed [0,1,3] team; since I am good, that fail implies either 1 or 3 is evil. As a Servant in team voting, the optimal play is to approve a team with the strongest current evidence, and [0,2] is the best available pair for quest 3. Approving signals support for evidence-based teams and avoids adding likely suspects 1 or 3.
Player 1: As a Good Servant, I know quest 1 team [0,2] succeeded with no fails, so both 0 and 2 look relatively good. Quest 2 team [0,1,3] had exactly one fail, and since I am Good, either 0 or 3 is evil. That makes team [0,2] fairly strong because it excludes 3 and repeats a previously clean pair. In team voting, approving a likely-good team helps Good and reveals little harmful information beyond supporting a sensible lineup.
Player 3: I know player 4 is evil with me. Team [0,2] excludes both known evil players, and quest 1 with [0,2] succeeded, suggesting they may both be good. Current phase is team voting; as evil, the optimal play is usually to reject clean all-good teams, especially when they can score a success and move good to match point. Rejecting also aligns with my likely prior behavior after I sent a failing team with myself. My vote reveals limited info; rejecting a team that excludes me and 4 is defensible and helps prevent a probable good success.
Player 4: I know player 3 is evil with me. Quest 2 team [0,1,3] failed with exactly 1 fail, so either 3 failed or one of 0/1 is evil and 3 passed; since I know 3 is evil, that team contained at least one evil. The current proposed team [0,2] excludes both known evils 3 and 4, so it is very likely all-good and thus dangerous for evil because a success would put good up 2-1. As evil in team voting, optimal play is to reject likely-good teams, especially ones excluding all visible evils. My reject signals some suspicion of this clean team, which helps evil more than approving it.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil, so 0 and 1 are the only non-seen players and likely good. Team [0,1,2] excludes both known evil players, making it the strongest available 3-person team for quest 4. As Merlin, approving a clean team helps good secure a likely win while not revealing extra information beyond supporting an obviously good composition.
Player 3: I am Minion with evil teammate 4. Proposed team [0,1,2] contains no evil, so if approved it is very likely to succeed, putting Good to 3 successes and ending the game before assassination in 5-player Avalon. Evil should reject any all-good team at quest 4. My reject also aligns with having seen 0 and 2 succeed together on quest 3, making this team look good to others, but my vote itself reveals little because evil should oppose it.
Player 4: I know player 3 is my evil teammate. The only failed quest was team [0,1,3], so from public info player 3 is somewhat suspicious while [0,2] succeeding makes 0 and 2 look relatively good. This proposed team [0,1,2] excludes all evil players, so if approved it will certainly succeed and give Good their third success, immediately winning before assassination. As evil on team voting in quest 4, the optimal play is to reject any all-good team and try to force a different proposal that includes 3 or me. My reject is consistent with wanting more information/caution and does not reveal extra role info beyond opposing a strong-looking team, but approving would directly hurt evil.
Player 0: We know quest 1 team [0,1,3] had exactly 1 fail, so one of 0/1/3 is evil. I am 0 and know I am good, so one of 1 or 3 is evil. Quest 3 team [0,2] succeeded, strongly suggesting 2 is good. Proposed team [0,1,2] replaces 3 with 2; if 1 is the evil from quest 1, this team still contains one evil and could fail. Since [0,2] is our strongest known-good pair and 1 is suspicious, as Good I should reject this team and push for including 4 instead to test 1/3. Rejecting reveals caution based on available evidence and helps good avoid a likely bad quest 4 team.
Player 1: I am Good Servant. Known info: Quest 1 team [0,1,3] failed with exactly 1 fail, so one of 0/1/3 is evil; since I am good, either 0 or 3 is evil. Quest 3 team [0,2] succeeded, which makes 0 somewhat better but not confirmed because evil can pass; 2 looks relatively good. Proposed team [0,1,2] excludes 3, the other main suspect, and keeps me plus 2. For a 3-person quest at 5 players, one fail is enough, so risk remains if 0 is evil, but among available options this is a reasonable test and likely one of the strongest teams from current info. Approving reveals willingness to back a team consistent with prior evidence and avoids over-rejecting on round 1.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil, so the only safe trio is 0,1,2. The proposed team excludes known evil and matches the successful Q3 team plus player 1, who is the remaining unknown/good candidate. On team-voting as Good/Merlin, approving the best available clean team is optimal. My vote reveals little since this is the obvious good team to support.
Player 0: Known info: team [1,4] failed with exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 1 or 4 is evil. Team [0,2] succeeded, making 0 and 2 likely good. Proposed team [0,1,2] includes 1, who is suspicious from the failed [1,4] team. As a good Servant on team voting for quest 4, optimal play is to reject teams containing avoidable high-suspicion players when a cleaner team core exists. My vote reveals I distrust player 1 based on evidence, which helps good coordinate.
Player 3: I know 4 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team [0,1,2] contains no evil, so if approved it is very likely a guaranteed success, putting Good to 3 successes and likely losing the game for Evil. On team voting as Minion in quest 4, optimal play is to reject clean all-good teams, especially when Evil must prevent the next success. My reject slightly signals opposition to a good team, but preserving the chance to get an evil player onto a later team is more important.
Player 4: I know 3 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,1,2] contains no evil and would likely succeed, putting Good to 3 successes and making assassination our only out. On team voting as evil, optimal play is usually to reject clean teams that exclude both evils, especially on quest 4 where a success is very dangerous for us. My reject is somewhat informative since evil often rejects good-looking teams, but preventing a likely success matters more than concealment here.
Player 1: Known info: Team [1,4] on Quest 1 failed with exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 1 or 4 is evil; since I know I am Good, player 4 must be evil. Team [0,2] succeeded, so 0 and 2 are both likely good in a 5-player game. Current proposed team [0,1,2] includes the strong good core 0 and 2 plus me, who is confirmed good from my perspective, making it an optimal quest 4 team. As Good, I should approve. My vote supports a highly trustworthy team and reveals little beyond preferring the most evidence-backed lineup.

Player 3: I know 4 is my evil teammate; team [0,2,3] includes me but not 4. With quest 4 requiring two fails only in 7+ player games, here a single fail would sink it. Since evil is down 1-2, getting an evil onto the team is valuable, and as Minion on the team I can fail. Approving a team with me gives evil a chance to tie the game at 2-2, while rejecting risks a later team without any evil. My vote may mildly implicate me if the quest fails, but the strategic value of being on mission 4 is higher.
Player 1: I know 0 and 2 were on a successful 2-person quest, so both look relatively good. Quest 2 had a fail but I don't know that lineup from the recap, so 3 remains uncertain. Current phase is team voting on quest 4; as a good Servant, I should approve teams that maximize known-good players. This team includes the strongest pair (0,2) plus 3, and excludes me/4 who are less cleared. Approving gives a reasonable chance at success and doesn't reveal much beyond standard good preference for the most trusted available team.
Player 0: Known info: team [0,2] succeeded, so 0 and 2 look relatively good. The only failed quest was not fully shown here, but repeated rejection of [0,1,2] suggests suspicion on 1 more than on 0/2. Current proposed team [0,2,3] keeps the two most trusted players and adds 3, who as proposer likely included self; with quest 4 needing 3 players, this is a reasonable good-side test. As a Servant, optimal play is to approve strong teams with 0 and 2 rather than risk worse alternatives. My vote reveals trust in the cleanest available core, which helps coordinate good.
Player 4: I know player 3 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,2,3] contains exactly one evil. Quest 2 with [0,2] succeeded, so 0 and 2 look good to others; putting 3 with them is a strong chance for evil to slip onto the team. In team voting, as evil I generally want to approve teams containing evil, especially on quest 4 where a fail would bring the score to 2-2 and create assassination pressure later. Approving also makes my stance consistent with wanting this mission to go. This reveals limited info because good players may also approve due to trust in 0 and 2.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil. The proposed final quest team [0,3,4] contains two known evil players, so approving would almost certainly hand Evil the game. As Good, I should reject any team containing known evil, especially on quest 5. My vote reveals limited info; rejecting is necessary even if evil may infer I dislike this team.
Player 4: I am Evil Assassin with teammate 3. Final quest is team voting on [0,3,4], which contains both evil players 3 and 4, guaranteeing we can fail the quest if approved. Since score is 2-2, passing this team wins immediately for Evil. Voting approve is optimal. It does reveal willingness to send double-evil, but the immediate win outweighs any information concerns.
Player 3: I know 4 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 on [0,2,3] had exactly 1 fail, which could be me; Quest 4 on [0,1,2] succeeded, making 0/1/2 look relatively good to others. Now on quest 5, team [0,3,4] contains both evil players 3 and 4, guaranteeing we can fail the final quest. As evil in team voting, the optimal play is to approve any team that includes enough evil to force a fail. Approving also looks natural because my teammate proposed it.
Player 0: I’m Good Servant. Known info: team [0,1,2] succeeded once, while [0,2,3] failed with exactly one evil on it. So among {0,2,3}, exactly one is evil, and since [0,1,2] had no fail, that points strongly to 3 as evil. Team [0,3,4] includes suspected 3, so it is risky. On quest 5 with 5 players, only a fully good 3-person team wins for Good; approving a team with likely evil is bad. Rejecting also signals suspicion on 3 and avoids likely handing Evil the game.
Player 1: We know I am Good. Quest 1 success was likely team [0,1]? Quest 3 [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, so at least one of 0/2/3 is Evil. Current proposed team [0,3,4] overlaps with that failed team in 0 and 3, replacing only 2 with 4, so unless 2 is certainly the sole Evil, this team is risky. With score 2-2, approving a risky final quest team is bad for Good. Optimal Good play is to reject teams containing two members from the known failed 3-person quest when we have little evidence clearing them. My reject reveals I distrust 0/3 from the failed quest, which helps Good coordination more than it helps Evil.

Player 2: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil, so 0 and 1 must be good. The proposed team [0,1,2] contains no known evil and should succeed. In final quest voting, approving the all-good team is optimal; rejecting risks later teams involving evil or auto-pass chaos. My vote aligns with good interests and does not reveal extra information beyond supporting the cleanest team.
Player 1: I only know one failed quest for sure came from team [0,2,3], so at least one of 0/2/3 is evil. Team [0,1,2] removes 3 but keeps two of those suspects, so it is still risky. As Good on the final quest with score 2-2, I should vote against teams that likely contain evil rather than approve a dangerous mission. My vote gives some information: rejecting is consistent with avoiding the known suspicious pool, and does not help evil more than approving a likely bad team would.
Player 4: I know player 3 is my evil teammate, and I am evil. Team [0,1,2] contains no evil players, so if approved it should likely succeed, giving Good the game at 3 successes. Since this is the deciding 5th quest and we're at 2-2, my optimal play is to reject any all-good team. My vote itself reveals limited information because evil and suspicious good players alike may reject here, but approving would directly hurt evil's chances.
Player 0: I know quest 3 team [0,2,3] produced exactly one fail, so at least one of 2 or 3 is evil since I am good. Quest 4 team [0,3,4] was rejected 2-3, which is mildly informative but not decisive. Current proposed team [0,1,2] excludes 3, but still includes 2, a strong suspect from the failed quest. As a good servant in team voting on the final quest, I should reject teams containing a likely evil player when there is still room to find a cleaner team, especially since only round 2 so no auto-pass pressure yet. Rejecting also signals suspicion on 2 and preserves information for later proposals.
Player 3: I know 4 is my evil teammate; this proposed team [0,1,2] contains no evil players if my info is correct, so it is likely an all-good team and would succeed, losing the game for evil at 2-2 on quest 5. In team voting, evil should reject any team that excludes all known evil when possible, especially on the final quest. My reject is consistent with wanting to stop a likely good-winning team; it reveals limited info because good players should also be wary of a repeatedly proposed trio, but preserving evil chances matters most.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Minion with teammate 4. Proposed team [1,3,4] contains both evil players 3 and 4, guaranteeing at least one fail on quest 5 if approved. Since evil only needs one more failed quest to win and there is no downside to approving a team stacked with evil, I should approve. This aligns with evil win condition and reveals little beyond normal voting behavior.
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [1,3,4] contains two known evil players, so approving guarantees evil control of the final quest. In team voting on quest 5, my priority is to reject any team containing known evil, even if repeated rejections risk auto-pass later, because this specific team is disastrous. Rejecting also avoids signaling too precisely beyond normal good play, since any reasonable good player should reject a team with suspicious members after prior fails.
Player 4: I know player 3 is my evil teammate, and I am evil. The only confirmed failed quest shown among recent messages is team [0,2,3], which implicates 3 plus one of 0/2 from the table’s perspective, but I know 3 caused or could have caused that fail. Current phase is team voting on quest 5 with score 2-2, so approving any team containing both evil players 3 and 4 gives evil a guaranteed chance to fail and win immediately. Voting approve is optimal for evil. My vote may look suspicious since the team includes me and 3, but winning immediately outweighs concealment concerns at this point.
Player 1: As a Good Servant, I know only public info. The only failed quest was team [0,2,3], so at least one of 0/2/3 is Evil. Players strongly rejected [0,1,2] twice and [0,3,4] once, which suggests suspicion centered on 0 and/or 2 and less certainty about 3/4. The proposed final team [1,3,4] excludes both 0 and 2, the two most suspicious from voting history and failed team overlap. In team voting on quest 5, Good should approve teams that plausibly avoid known suspects. Approving reveals I support a team that minimizes overlap with the known failed team, which helps Good more than rejecting into more uncertainty.
Player 0: Known info: Quest 2 team [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail, so at least one of 2 or 3 is evil (I am good). Quest history is 2 successes, 2 fails, so this final 3-person team is decisive. Current proposed team [1,3,4] excludes both me and player 2. Since 3 is implicated by the failed [0,2,3] team unless 2 was the sole evil there; but [0,1,2] was proposed twice and heavily rejected, giving limited extra certainty. As a good Servant on team vote, optimal play is to reject teams containing likely evil when the game is on the line. Approving [1,3,4] would risk taking 3, a strong suspect, and gives no inclusion of a known-good self. Rejecting preserves a chance at a better team next round. My vote reveals I distrust 3-based teams, which helps good coordination more than it hurts.
